---
title: Roasting coffee at home
date: 2024-02-17
tags: [cooking, coffee, roasting]
---

I started with a cast iron skillet and a wooden spoon before upgrading to a small drum roaster. Green beans keep for a year or more in a cool cupboard, so I buy a few kilos at a time from a local importer and roast a batch every week.

The beans pass through a yellow hay-like stage, then brown, then emit a sharp crackling sound called first crack. Pulling them at the end of first crack gives a bright, acidic cup; riding a little further into the quiet zone produces something rounder and sweeter. Second crack is louder and oilier, and for my palate that is too far.

Freshly roasted beans need a day or two to degas before they brew well. I store them in a one-way valve bag and grind immediately before use. The difference between supermarket coffee and a bean roasted three days ago is hard to overstate.
